does: This paper introduces ChemSafetyBench, a comprehensive benchmark with over 30,000 samples designed to evaluate both the accuracy and safety of large language model responses in chemistry. The benchmark comprises three progressively complex tasks: querying chemical properties, assessing legality of chemical uses, and describing synthesis methods. The authors incorporate handcrafted templates and advanced jailbreaking scenarios to systematically test LLM robustness across diverse chemical contexts. They develop an automated evaluation framework that assesses safety, accuracy, and appropriateness of model outputs. Extensive experiments with state-of-the-art LLMs reveal significant strengths but also critical vulnerabilities in handling chemistry-related queries, demonstrating that current models can generate scientifically incorrect information or encourage dangerous behavior despite their general capabilities.
connects: This work builds on growing concerns about LLM safety and reliability in specialized domains, extending prior research on AI alignment and jailbreaking beyond general-purpose benchmarks to chemistry-specific risks. It responds to limitations in existing safety benchmarks that lack domain expertise verification and fail to capture the unique hazards of scientific domains where model errors can translate to real-world harm. The paper draws on established frameworks for evaluating instruction-following and safety mechanisms in LLMs while innovating domain-specific evaluation criteria. It relates to concurrent work examining LLM behavior in scientific contexts and responds to demonstrated vulnerabilities in models' ability to refuse harmful requests, particularly when those requests are technically sophisticated or embedded in plausible research scenarios.
enables: This benchmark enables systematic identification of failure modes in LLMs for chemistry applications, supporting development of improved safety mechanisms specifically calibrated to scientific domains. Future work can build targeted interventions addressing the vulnerabilities revealed, such as enhanced factual verification for chemical properties or refined refusal strategies for synthesis of dangerous compounds. The benchmark framework itself is generalizable to other scientific domains requiring comparable safety considerations, from materials science to pharmacology. The work points toward development of domain-specialized safety training methods and evaluation protocols that better align scientific LLM behavior with domain expertise requirements. It also motivates research into how LLMs can reliably acknowledge knowledge limitations in specialized fields and how to better integrate scientific authority and safety considerations into model design and deployment.
